explanation.text: Neil's visits to the planetarium and the dark Pennsylvania sky make him realize how many real stars exist. Saying the universe was "calling him" means these experiences inspired him to learn more about astronomy.

Paragraph 3 says Neil wanted to know more about the stars after seeing them clearly. Paragraph 6 supports that idea because he uses binoculars, studies the moon's craters, and wants an even better view.
Neil's parents buy his first telescope and later pay half the cost of a more powerful one after he saves the rest. These actions show that they support his interest in studying the sky.

Neil takes astronomy courses, studies with his telescope, chooses a science high school, and attends space camp. These choices show how he turns an early interest in stars into the beginning of a career as an astrophysicist.

The passage mainly traces Neil's lasting interest in the universe and the work he does to become an astrophysicist. Taking his telescope to the roof to study the night sky directly supports that main idea.

Neil walks dogs and saves money for a stronger telescope, then continues taking courses and studying the sky. His repeated effort demonstrates that he is serious about reaching his goal of becoming an astrophysicist.

Hayley practices before the tryouts, and Mr. Y gives her a thumbs-up and tells her she is in the show. This evidence indicates that she is chosen because she plays the ukulele well enough to pass the tryout.

Hayley's parents cheer when she makes the show, and her father confidently predicts that she will be a star. These reactions are important because they show that her family believes in and supports her.
The story mentions two fifth graders, some fourth graders, and a kindergartener among the acts. This evidence confirms that students from different grades perform in the talent show.

The illustration shows Hayley onstage playing her ukulele and making a big performing gesture before a cheering crowd. This scene matches paragraph 13, which describes her song and her guitar-hero arm swing.
After Hayley performs, other students tell her she is cool and ask if they can join her band. Their response shows that her successful performance earns their respect for her musical ability.

Hayley first feels sick with fear and wants to escape, but she takes a breath, becomes confident, and performs. The applause that follows demonstrates the central message that overcoming fear can lead to success.

Most animals look through the chain-link wall, but Nitro sits facing the concrete walls and follows voices instead of movements. His failure to look outside the cage is Kathryn's first clue because it suggests that he cannot see what is beyond it.

The passage centers on the caretakers' challenge of teaching a blind tiger to move safely through a large new home. The statement in paragraph 8 best expresses that main idea because it names both Nitro's problem and the help he needs.

Kathryn worries when Nitro walks into the fence and hurts his nose, then helps create scent markers to protect him. Her desire to keep him from injuring himself shows that she cares about his well-being.

The problem in paragraph 8 is that Nitro cannot use sight to find his way through the new cage. Learning where every bump, tree, and food box is shows that he has solved that problem and can navigate his home.

By paragraph 16, Nitro knows the location of everything in his cage and can even locate people who stand still. This shows that he has "mastered" the space because he can move through it confidently and comfortably.
Nitro knows when people are nearby even though he cannot see them. He chuffles toward them to make them answer, which lets him recognize who is there by the person's voice.

Visitors always stop at Nitro's cage, and he responds by coming to the fence and happily chuffling toward people. This friendly, dependable reaction to visitors explains why he has become a favorite at the Rescue.
